nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef zones and rock substrates in the photic zone
nativeRange.biotope: Coastal rocky reef, hard substrate attachment

decorPreferences: Rock attachment via wedging between rockwork
Zip-tie or fishing line to frag plug or live rock

avoidWith: Herbivorous fish that graze heavily on macroalgae (tangs, rabbitfish)
Strong turbulent flow that may dislodge specimens

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product image from Mosaic Macros listing. Shows feathery plume form.
Secondary product image from Mosaic Macros listing.

summary: The Sea Plume (Asparagopsis taxiformis) is a delicate, feathery red macroalga prized for its ornamental plume form. It is notable in scientific research for producing halogenated compounds linked to livestock methane reduction. Populations have become invasive in the Mediterranean and parts of the temperate Pacific.
careNotes:
The Sea Plume is a delicate ornamental red alga with fuzzy, intricately-branching growth that gives the plant its distinctive feathery appearance. Coloration is variable — typically pale reddish-pink or cream, though red, orange, and tan forms have been observed. Under good conditions it tends to form dense, bushy colonies. Turbulent but not excessive flow is generally advisable; too much current can tear the delicate fronds.

Rock attachment is required. As a red alga, the Sea Plume will melt at the base if buried in sand, so it should never be placed directly in a sand bed. Attachment options include wedging the base between rockwork (preferred), securing with zip-ties or fishing line to a frag plug, or using superglue gel directly to a rock surface. Actinic-only lighting is generally not sufficient; full-spectrum LED is preferred.

Water parameters should be kept broadly in line with standard reef chemistry: temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, nitrate 5–20 ppm, and phosphate 0.02–0.1 ppm.

The Sea Plume has attracted scientific interest for its biology beyond the aquarium: it is well-documented as a source of bromoform and other halogenated organic compounds, and research has shown that supplementing cattle feed with small quantities of this alga can significantly reduce methane emissions from livestock — a finding that has drawn considerable attention for its potential role in reducing agricultural greenhouse gas output.

IMPORTANT — INVASIVE SPECIES: Asparagopsis taxiformis has established invasive populations in the Mediterranean Sea and parts of the temperate Pacific, including coastal zones in California and elsewhere, where it outcompetes native algal communities. Aquarists in affected regions should take care to prevent any discharge of tank water or algal material into local waterways.
breedingNotes: In the wild, Asparagopsis taxiformis has a heteromorphic alternation of generations life cycle — the feathery gametophyte (the form sold in the aquarium trade) alternates with a smaller crustose sporophyte stage (Falkenbergia phase). In the aquarium context, propagation is generally accomplished by fragmentation: healthy fronds can be carefully divided and attached to new rock surfaces. Successful gametophyte-to-sporophyte cycling in captivity has not been widely documented in the hobby literature.
